title: Pakistan Strikes Sayed Jamaluddin Afghani University in Asadabad — 4–7 Killed, 70–85 Wounded; Pakistan Denies; Chaman-Spin Boldak Clashes; OCHA Update #4
detail:
April 28, 2026 marked a major escalation in the Afghanistan-Pakistan conflict as Pakistani mortar and missile strikes struck civilian neighborhoods and the Sayed Jamaluddin Afghani University in Asadabad, the capital of Kunar province in eastern Afghanistan. Afghan authorities reported 4–7 people killed and 70–85 wounded, including approximately 30 students and professors. Among the wounded were 13 women and 39 children. Taliban Deputy Spokesperson Hamdullah Fitrat denounced the strikes as 'unforgivable war crimes against civilians and academic institutions,' calling on the international community to respond. Afghanistan's Ministry of Higher Education confirmed structural damage to the university. Al Jazeera and CNN aired footage from the strike scene showing damaged buildings and wounded civilians being evacuated.

Pakistan's Ministry of Information categorically denied all strikes, calling Afghan claims 'a blatant lie,' 'frivolous and fake,' and accusing Afghan media of running 'fake news to cover up support for terrorist groups.' Pakistan's Express Tribune, citing security sources, reported a lower casualty figure of 3 killed and 45 wounded at the university, while still deflecting responsibility. Pakistan's denial represents its consistent strategy of attributing Afghan civilian casualties to TTP staging or fabrication throughout Operation Ghazab-il-Haq.

Separately, Pakistani security forces reported responding to what they described as 'unprovoked firing' by Afghan Taliban forces in the Zolul Khel / Angoor Adda area of South Waziristan — the same sector where Afghan Taliban cross-border artillery struck on April 27. Pakistani sources claimed to have destroyed several Afghan border posts; 3 Pakistani civilians were wounded in the exchange.

In the Kandahar-Balochistan border zone, clashes at the Chaman (Pakistan) / Spin Boldak (Afghanistan) crossing continued. Taliban Deputy Spokesperson Fitrat stated Pakistani shelling in Spin Boldak district killed 5 people, including one Taliban member.

UN OCHA published its 4th Afghanistan Situation Update on the humanitarian impact of the Pakistan-Afghanistan military escalation on April 28. Key humanitarian figures: approximately 160,000 Afghans facing heightened food insecurity; approximately 50,000 at risk of gender-based violence; 19 health facilities closed or operating at reduced capacity affecting an estimated 78,000 people. OCHA expressed concern that the conflict's continuation was systematically destroying health infrastructure in Kunar, Nangarhar, Nuristan, and other eastern provinces.

The university strike, if confirmed, would represent one of the most symbolically significant attacks of the conflict — striking a functioning civilian academic institution in a provincial capital in front of international media, during what is nominally a China-mediated non-escalation period. The Taliban government filed a formal diplomatic protest with China, which has positioned itself as the guarantor of the Urumqi framework. As of end of day April 28, China's foreign ministry had not publicly commented on the incident.

media.caption: Pakistan strikes Sayed Jamaluddin Afghani University in Asadabad, Kunar — Taliban calls it 'war crime against civilians and academic institutions'; Pakistan denies
OCHA Situation Update #4: ~160,000 Afghans face heightened food insecurity; 19 health facilities closed or reduced; 78,000 people affected
Pakistan denies cross-border strikes as 'blatant lie' while Afghan sources report 4 killed in Kunar province
